choroidal_neovascularization: OCT image showing choroidal neovascularization, characterized by abnormal blood vessel growth beneath the retina.
Choroidal neovascularization appears as hyperreflective lesions with subretinal or intraretinal fluid on OCT.
The image may reveal pigment epithelial detachment and disruption of normal retinal layers.
This condition is commonly associated with age-related macular degeneration and can lead to vision loss.
Choroidal neovascularization is identified by the presence of new, leaky vessels beneath the retinal pigment epithelium.
diabetic_macular_edema: OCT image showing diabetic macular edema, characterized by retinal thickening and cystoid spaces in the macula.
Diabetic macular edema appears as hyporeflective cystic areas and increased retinal thickness on OCT.
The image may show diffuse or focal swelling of the central retina due to fluid accumulation.
This condition is a common complication of diabetic retinopathy and can impair central vision.
Diabetic macular edema is identified by the presence of intraretinal fluid and cystoid changes in the macula.
drusen: OCT image showing drusen, which are yellowish deposits beneath the retinal pigment epithelium.
Drusen appear as small, dome-shaped elevations of the RPE layer on OCT scans.
The image may reveal multiple discrete mounds or bumps under the retina, typical of early macular degeneration.
Drusen are associated with age-related macular degeneration and may precede more severe retinal changes.
This class is characterized by the presence of subretinal deposits without significant fluid or neovascularization.
normal_OCT_scan: OCT image of a healthy retina, showing normal anatomical layers and no evidence of pathology.
A normal OCT scan displays well-defined retinal layers with no signs of fluid, thickening, or abnormal deposits.
There are no features of neovascularization, edema, or drusen in a normal scan.
The image serves as a reference for comparison with pathological OCT findings.
Normal OCT scans reveal intact retinal structure and regular reflectivity patterns throughout the macula.
